key_evidence: MS Defender explicitly categorized this as InitialAccess with MITRE technique T1566.002 (Spearphishing Link), indicating a malicious link delivered via email.
EntityType=MailMessage confirms an email-based delivery vector; a specific user account is referenced via AccountUpn, AccountSid, AccountObjectId, and AccountName fields.

Related evidence includes OAuthApplicationId and RegistryKey/RegistryValueName/RegistryValueData fields — suggesting possible post-click activity (e.g., OAuth consent phishing or malware persistence) beyond mere email receipt.

All IOC field values (Sha256, IpAddress, Url, FileName, DeviceId, AccountName, etc.) are internal numeric reference IDs, not resolved artifacts — actual hashes, URLs, and account identities cannot be determined from this export and must be retrieved from the Defender portal.
Alert fired at 01:36 UTC (approx. 22:36 local, outside business hours), which is outside normal working hours and warrants additional scrutiny.
summary: MS Defender raised a confirmed InitialAccess alert tied to a spearphishing link (T1566.002) delivered via email to at least one internal user account. The presence of related evidence referencing OAuthApplicationId and registry artifacts alongside the mail message entity is a significant concern — it may indicate the link led to an OAuth consent grant or malware installation, elevating this above a simple phishing receipt. Critically, all IOC values in this alert export are unresolved internal numeric identifiers; a Tier 2 analyst must open this alert directly in the Microsoft Defender XDR/Sentinel portal to retrieve the actual account UPN, the URL clicked, the SHA256 hash, the device name, and any associated timeline events. Priority actions: identify the impacted user, revoke any suspicious OAuth grants, and review the device timeline for post-click activity.
